content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob have an array of AAA of NNN elements, and will play a game on it. They alternate turns, with Alice going first.

On each turn:


Let MMM denote the current length of AAA.

If M=1M = 1M=1, the game stops. Otherwise, the current player must make a move.
On Alice's turn, she must choose integers LLL and RRR (1ÔëĄL<RÔëĄM1 \leq L \lt R \leq M1ÔëĄL<RÔëĄM), delete the elements AL,AL+1,ÔÇŽ,ARA_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_RALÔÇő,AL+1ÔÇő,ÔÇŽ,ARÔÇő from the array,

and insert ÔłúAL+AL+1+ÔÇŽ+ARÔłú|A_L + A_{L+1} + \ldots + A_R|ÔłúALÔÇő+AL+1ÔÇő+ÔÇŽ+ARÔÇőÔłú in their place.

Note that ÔłúxÔłú|x|ÔłúxÔłú denotes the absolute value of xxx.
On Bob's turn, he must choose integers LLL and RRR (1ÔëĄL<RÔëĄM1 \leq L \lt R \leq M1ÔëĄL<RÔëĄM), delete the elements AL,AL+1,ÔÇŽ,ARA_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_RALÔÇő,AL+1ÔÇő,ÔÇŽ,ARÔÇő from the array, and insert (AL+AL+1+ÔÇŽ+AR)(A_L + A_{L+1} + \ldots + A_R)(ALÔÇő+AL+1ÔÇő+ÔÇŽ+ARÔÇő) in their place.
That is, Alice must choose some subarray, delete it, and insert the absolute value of its sum in its place.

Bob will do the same, but insert just the sum (and not its absolute value).

Note that since L<RL \lt RL<R, the length of the array will decrease after each move.

Alice wants to maximize the value of the final element, while Bob wants to minimize it.

If both Alice and Bob make their moves optimally, what will the value of the final element be?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN ÔÇö the number of elements in AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,ÔÇŽ,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1ÔÇő,A2ÔÇő,ÔÇŽ,ANÔÇő, denoting the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the value of the final element if both players move optimally.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 4Ôőů1054 \cdot 10^54Ôőů105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The array is already of length 111, so no moves will be made.
Test case 222: Since L<RL \lt RL<R, Alice has no choice but to choose the entire array [5,Ôłĺ7][5, -7][5,Ôłĺ7] and replace it with Ôłú5+(Ôłĺ7)Ôłú=2|5 + (-7)| = 2Ôłú5+(Ôłĺ7)Ôłú=2, at which point the game ends.
Test case 333: One possible sequence of moves is as follows:

Alice chooses L=5L = 5L=5 and R=6R = 6R=6, replacing the subarray [Ôłĺ2,Ôłĺ1][-2, -1][Ôłĺ2,Ôłĺ1] with its absolute sum 333.
The array is now A=[1,1,Ôłĺ2,5,3]A = [1, 1, -2, 5, 3]A=[1,1,Ôłĺ2,5,3].
Bob chooses L=2L = 2L=2 and R=4R = 4R=4, replacing the subarray [1,Ôłĺ2,5][1, -2, 5][1,Ôłĺ2,5] with its sum 444.
The array is now A=[1,4,3]A = [1, 4, 3]A=[1,4,3].
Alice chooses L=2L = 2L=2 and R=3R = 3R=3, turning the array into A=[1,7]A = [1, 7]A=[1,7].
Bob chooses L=1L = 1L=1 and R=2R = 2R=2, turning the array into A=[8]A = [8]A=[8].
